question: [File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged maker who is the sell-side of an offer deploy a reverting ISellCallback contract as offer.callback under the precondition that the offer is live and partially filled, trigger take() -> ISellCallback.onSell() -> revert, violating the invariant that a valid taker with correct assets must always be able to fill a live offer, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS of take() for all takers of that offer at zero marginal cost to the maker? Proof idea: deploy a callback that reverts on onSell, sign a sell offer with offer.callback set to it, call take() as taker, assert WrongSellCallbackReturnValue is always thrown regardless of taker input.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged taker supply a reverting IBuyCallback as takerCallback under the precondition that the offer is a sell offer (offer.buy == false) and the taker has sufficient assets, trigger take() -> IBuyCallback.onBuy() -> revert, violating the invariant that a maker's sell offer must remain fillable by any authorized taker, causing scoped impact: taker-side DoS of take() for any offer where the taker controls the callback? Proof idea: deploy a callback that reverts on onBuy, call take() with takerCallback set to it, assert WrongBuyCallbackReturnValue is always thrown.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker who is authorized on behalf of a borrower (seller) call supplyCollateral() to activate a collateral slot whose oracle always reverts, under the precondition that the seller has existing debt and fewer than MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER (16) active collaterals, trigger take() -> isHealthy() -> IOracle.price() -> revert at line 953, violating the invariant that a seller with healthy collateral must be able to participate in take(), causing scoped impact: permanent DoS of take() for that seller at the cost of one supplyCollateral call? Proof idea: deploy a reverting oracle, supply 1 wei of that collateral to the seller, call take() as any taker against a sell offer from that seller, assert revert.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker who is an authorized operator of the maker call setConsumed(offer.group, type(uint256).max) under the precondition that the offer has maxUnits > 0 and consumed[maker][group] < maxUnits, trigger take() -> consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] += units -> require(newConsumed <= offer.maxUnits) -> revert with ConsumedUnits, violating the invariant that a maker's offer must remain fillable until its stated capacity is reached, causing scoped impact: low-cost front-run DoS of any take() against that offer group? Proof idea: fuzz the timing of setConsumed vs take, assert ConsumedUnits is thrown after setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max).

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged market creator create a market with an enterGate whose canIncreaseCredit() always reverts under the precondition that any taker attempts a buy-side take that increases buyerCreditIncrease > 0, trigger take() -> IEnterGate(offer.market.enterGate).canIncreaseCredit(buyer) -> revert, violating the invariant that a valid market must allow credit increases for eligible buyers, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS of all credit-increasing takes in that market at market creation cost? Proof idea: deploy a reverting IEnterGate, create market with it, attempt take() with buyerCreditIncrease > 0, assert BuyerGatedFromIncreasingCredit.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged market creator create a market with an enterGate whose canIncreaseDebt() always reverts under the precondition that any taker attempts a sell-side take that increases sellerDebtIncrease > 0, trigger take() -> IEnterGate(offer.market.enterGate).canIncreaseDebt(seller) -> revert, violating the invariant that a valid market must allow debt increases for eligible sellers, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS of all debt-increasing takes in that market? Proof idea: deploy a reverting IEnterGate.canIncreaseDebt, create market, attempt take() with sellerDebtIncrease > 0, assert SellerGatedFromIncreasingDebt.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker trigger repeated bad-debt liquidations to drive marketState[id].lossFactor to type(uint128).max under the precondition that the market has accumulated bad debt across multiple positions, trigger take() -> require(_marketState.lossFactor < type(uint128).max) -> revert with MarketLossFactorMaxedOut, violating the invariant that a market with outstanding offers must remain tradeable, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS of all take() calls in that market? Proof idea: fuzz bad-debt liquidation sequences, assert that once lossFactor == type(uint128).max, take() always reverts.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls an oracle manipulate the oracle price downward just before a take() call under the precondition that the seller has debt and the post-take health check at line 476 is borderline, trigger take() -> state updates -> isHealthy() -> oracle.price() returns low value -> maxDebt < debt -> revert with SellerIsLiquidatable, violating the invariant that a healthy seller must be able to participate in take(), causing scoped impact: DoS of take() for any seller whose health depends on a manipulable oracle? Proof idea: fuzz oracle price around the health threshold, assert SellerIsLiquidatable is thrown when price drops below threshold post-take.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a malicious IRatifier that always reverts on isRatified() and get the maker to authorize it via isAuthorized[maker][ratifier] = true under the precondition that the offer references this ratifier, trigger take() -> IRatifier(offer.ratifier).isRatified() -> revert, violating the invariant that a ratifier-authorized offer must be fillable, causing scoped impact: DoS of take() for all offers using that ratifier? Proof idea: deploy reverting IRatifier, authorize it, sign offer with it, call take(), assert RatifierFail.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the liquidation lock mechanism by being the taker of a sell offer with a long-running ISellCallback that reenters take() on a different offer under the precondition that the seller of the outer take has debt, trigger outer take() -> tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) -> ISellCallback.onSell() -> inner take() -> inner isHealthy() -> inner oracle call, violating the invariant that the liquidation lock must not allow nested takes to bypass health checks, causing scoped impact: DoS or state corruption via nested take during sell callback? Proof idea: implement a sell callback that reenters take(), assert that the inner take either reverts or leaves the seller in an inconsistent state.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker who is the maker of a buy offer set offer.callback to a contract that reenters repay() during IBuyCallback.onBuy() under the precondition that the buyer has existing debt, trigger take() -> IBuyCallback.onBuy() -> repay() -> position[id][buyer].debt -= units, violating the invariant that partial state updates during take callbacks must not be exploitable, causing scoped impact: DoS of take() if repay causes debt underflow or state inconsistency that fails the post-take health check? Proof idea: implement a buy callback that calls repay() on the buyer, assert that the outer take reverts or leaves accounting inconsistent.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the payer assignment logic at line 422 (payer = buyerCallback != address(0) ? buyerCallback : ...) by providing a buyerCallback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS but has no token balance and no approval under the precondition that buyerAssets > 0, trigger take() -> IBuyCallback.onBuy() returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> revert, violating the invariant that a successful callback must guarantee token delivery, causing scoped impact: DoS of take() when payer is a callback with insufficient balance? Proof idea: deploy a callback that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS but holds no tokens, assert safeTransferFrom reverts.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: take] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls the loan token deploy a fee-on-transfer ERC20 as the market's loan token under the precondition that the fee causes the actual received amount to differ from buyerAssets, trigger take() -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> token charges fee -> Midnight receives less than expected, violating the invariant that ERC20 transfer deltas must match accounting deltas, causing scoped impact: accounting desync that causes subsequent withdraw() or claimContinuousFee() to revert due to insufficient balance? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer token, create market, execute take(), assert Midnight balance < withdrawable + continuousFeeCredit.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: repay] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a malicious IRepayCallback that always reverts on onRepay() and convince a borrower to use it under the precondition that the borrower calls repay() with callback set to the malicious contract, trigger repay() -> IRepayCallback(callback).onRepay() -> revert with WrongRepayCallbackReturnValue, violating the invariant that a borrower must always be able to repay their debt, causing scoped impact: DoS of repay() for any borrower who uses a malicious or buggy callback? Proof idea: deploy reverting IRepayCallback, call repay() with it, assert WrongRepayCallbackReturnValue.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: repay] Can an unprivileged attacker who is an authorized operator of a borrower call repay() on behalf of the borrower to reduce position[id][borrower].debt to 0 under the precondition that the borrower has a pending repay() transaction in the mempool, trigger front-run repay() -> position[id][onBehalf].debt = 0 -> borrower's repay() -> position[id][onBehalf].debt -= units -> arithmetic underflow revert, violating the invariant that a borrower must always be able to repay their own debt, causing scoped impact: DoS of borrower's repay() via authorized operator front-run? Proof idea: simulate front-run by calling repay(onBehalf=borrower) as authorized operator before borrower's tx, assert borrower's repay underflows.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: repay] Can an unprivileged attacker cause marketState[id].withdrawable to overflow uint128 by calling repay() with units close to type(uint128).max under the precondition that withdrawable is already near type(uint128).max, trigger repay() -> marketState[id].withdrawable += UtilsLib.toUint128(units) -> CastOverflow revert in toUint128, violating the invariant that repay must always succeed for valid debt amounts, causing scoped impact: DoS of repay() when withdrawable is near the uint128 ceiling? Proof idea: fuzz units and initial withdrawable values near type(uint128).max, assert CastOverflow is thrown.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: repay] Can an unprivileged attacker who is an authorized operator of a borrower call setIsAuthorized() to authorize a malicious contract, then have that contract call repay() with a reverting IRepayCallback under the precondition that the borrower has debt, trigger repay() -> IRepayCallback.onRepay() -> revert, violating the invariant that authorization must not enable DoS of the authorizer's own repay path, causing scoped impact: DoS of repay() via coarse-grained authorization abuse? Proof idea: authorize malicious operator, have it call repay() with reverting callback, assert borrower cannot repay.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: withdraw] Can an unprivileged attacker drain marketState[id].withdrawable below a lender's credit by calling liquidate() with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0 (bad debt realization) under the precondition that a borrower has bad debt and withdrawable is exactly equal to the lender's credit, trigger liquidate() -> _marketState.totalUnits -= badDebt -> withdrawable unchanged but totalUnits decreases -> subsequent withdraw() -> _marketState.withdrawable -= units -> underflow revert, violating the invariant that a lender with credit must always be able to withdraw, causing scoped impact: DoS of withdraw() for lenders after bad debt realization? Proof idea: realize bad debt, assert withdrawable < lender credit, call withdraw(), assert underflow.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: withdraw] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the _updatePosition() call in withdraw() to slash a lender's credit below the requested units under the precondition that the market has a high lossFactor and the lender's lastLossFactor is stale, trigger withdraw() -> _updatePosition() -> newCredit = credit * (MAX - lossFactor) / (MAX - lastLossFactor) -> credit slashed to value < units -> _position.credit -= units -> arithmetic underflow revert, violating the invariant that a lender must be able to withdraw their post-slash credit, causing scoped impact: DoS of withdraw() for lenders with stale positions after bad debt? Proof idea: fuzz lossFactor and lastLossFactor values, assert that withdraw(units) reverts when units > post-slash credit.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: withdraw] Can an unprivileged attacker cause withdraw() to revert by manipulating the pendingFee calculation at line 490 under the precondition that _position.credit > 0 and _position.pendingFee > 0 and units == _position.credit (full withdrawal), trigger withdraw() -> pendingFeeDecrease = pendingFee.mulDivUp(units, credit) -> pendingFeeDecrease > pendingFee -> _position.pendingFee -= pendingFeeDecrease -> arithmetic underflow, violating the invariant that a full credit withdrawal must always succeed, causing scoped impact: DoS of withdraw() for lenders attempting full withdrawal? Proof idea: fuzz credit, pendingFee, and units values near equality, assert underflow when mulDivUp rounds up pendingFeeDecrease above pendingFee.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: withdraw] Can an unprivileged attacker who is an authorized operator of a lender call withdraw() to drain the lender's credit to 0 under the precondition that the lender has a pending withdraw() transaction in the mempool, trigger front-run withdraw() -> _position.credit = 0 -> lender's withdraw() -> _position.credit -= units -> arithmetic underflow revert, violating the invariant that a lender must always be able to withdraw their own credit, causing scoped impact: DoS of lender's withdraw() via authorized operator front-run? Proof idea: simulate front-run by calling withdraw(onBehalf=lender) as authorized operator, assert lender's withdraw underflows.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged attacker supply a collateral with a reverting oracle to a borrower's position under the precondition that the borrower has fewer than MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER active collaterals and the attacker is authorized on behalf of the borrower, trigger liquidate() -> while (_collateralBitmap != 0) -> IOracle(_collateralParam.oracle).price() -> revert, violating the invariant that an unhealthy borrower must always be liquidatable, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS of liquidate() for that borrower at the cost of one supplyCollateral call? Proof idea: deploy reverting oracle, supply 1 wei of that collateral to borrower, call liquidate(), assert revert.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged attacker who is the taker of a sell offer hold the liquidation lock on a borrower (seller) by executing a take() with a long-running ISellCallback under the precondition that the borrower is the seller in the offer and is unhealthy, trigger take() -> tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) -> ISellCallback.onSell() -> liquidate() -> require(!liquidationLocked(id, borrower)) -> revert with NotLiquidatable, violating the invariant that an unhealthy borrower must be liquidatable at any time, causing scoped impact: temporary DoS of liquidate() during take callbacks? Proof idea: implement a sell callback that calls liquidate() on the seller, assert NotLiquidatable is thrown.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a malicious ILiquidateCallback that always reverts on onLiquidate() under the precondition that a liquidator uses this callback, trigger liquidate() -> ILiquidateCallback(callback).onLiquidate() -> revert with WrongLiquidateCallbackReturnValue, violating the invariant that a liquidator must always be able to liquidate an unhealthy position, causing scoped impact: DoS of liquidate() for any liquidator using a malicious or buggy callback? Proof idea: deploy reverting ILiquidateCallback, call liquidate() with it, assert WrongLiquidateCallbackReturnValue.

[File: 'src/Midnight.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: liquidate] Can an unprivileged market creator create a market with a liquidatorGate whose canLiquidate() always reverts under the precondition that any liquidator attempts to liquidate a borrower in that market, trigger liquidate() -> ILiquidatorGate(market.liquidatorGate).canLiquidate(msg.sender) -> revert, violating the invariant that unhealthy positions must be liquidatable, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS of liquidate() in that market at market creation cost? Proof idea: deploy reverting ILiquidatorGate, create market with it, call liquidate(), assert LiquidatorGatedFromLiquidating.
